item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just found this article and I absolutely love how cryptic it is! Its really strange and surreal and gives you just enough to vaguely understand what is happening while also leaving you with so little that you go nuts trying to figure it out. That final paragraph is upsetting as hell and I fully went over the edge when I realized that the process of removing eyes could be applied on a metaphysical narrative level.

Easy +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have no idea what exactly is going on, but I love it!
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The only article necessary for this one is crosslinked to at the postorbital lobes mention, in which the only important part is what the postorbital lobes are. The rest is standalone. Any mystery and/or confusion beyond that is intentional.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting skip, but I really don't like when story is impossible to understand without reading 2137 tales and 15 canons before. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm… really conflicted on this one. On one hand the story itself isn't bad but the core of SCP is and always has been the SCP itself. Its containment procedure is waved off with one sentence and the SCP itself is never described.

Many of the best SCPs on this site are just descriptions of the anomalous object, even the ones that do have exploration logs or addendums tend to center them around the anomaly itself. very very few center them around the characters and even fewer do it in a way that resonates well. I will admit, I enjoyed the characters in this SCP but not enough to justify how barebones the anomaly itself is. The tone also seems to lack that clinical, scientific vibe and lingo that is present in most other SCP articles. This feels less like an SCP and honestly more like an SCP tale that should be supplementary material to the SCP article itself.

The story itself was also a little confusing for me, I like the concept of what this SCP can do but I think it could've been handled much better. Even a heavily expunged and censored description of the process to remove the eye would at least be something, or maybe some kind of interesting containment procedure? In fact many story and character centered articles use the containment and description sections to build on tension. This feels more like you threw that away because you wanted to write about your characters first and foremost, and that would be perfectly fine if it was a tale. But it isn't. I'm going to have to downvote this one. Great concept, decent execution if it was a tale, but as an SCP it is simply lackluster.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I mean, I don't know what else I'm supposed to say? Excluding the steps was a decision on my part when I was writing, so I wanted to make that clear in my response. If you dislike that then that's fine; I'm not going to argue that someone has to like what I write.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It honestly seems more likely that they'd store the ritual in an external file/database that couldn't be accessed without the correct permissions.

More importantly, though, I just… don't understand where you're coming from? Like, you're reading fiction and the fiction would be disrupted by the inclusion of an extraneous detail like that, so it was removed. That's how fiction works. If SCPs strove to be actual scientific documents they'd be nigh-unreadable and mind-numbingly boring.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
novoting. author comments in this very section would make me downvote it if not for the fact that it's clearly able to affect recordings / fiction / metanarratives. that's a great way to earn brownie points from me. but things like

"I didn't consider any of the steps as being necessary for the story. This isn't about how the anomaly works; it's about how it can be exploited, how it was exploited."

are a great way to lose those same brownie points. the Foundation would absolutely put the steps to perform this ritual in the SCP documentation — even if it's redacted.

the net result is a novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Your description doesn’t even begin to mention it’s reality changing effects. I get that it’s a build up to the story but the description should atleast allude to everything the scip can do or it isn’t really a description. From actually reading just the description, all I could get is that somehow an eye can be taken out and replaced and that surface will close around it to make a socket. Which also isn’t described in any of the testing logs. If it changes the topography of the surface the first testing log should describe how to surface wrapped around the eye.

The testing logs show how the foundation came to know it’s properties, if it isn’t really described then that information just seems to be coming out of no where

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have no idea what's going on and I'm okay with that. A wonderfully bizarre scip. +1

(I also learned about non-binary pronouns, so that's cool. Gotta admit those threw me for a sec there.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
lmao

even in my fiction my trans characters name themselves after anime

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I knew EXACTLY who wrote this the second I saw the "Langley" reference.

+1, it's great as always.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i love the music you put in the discussion post its great

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yep; just fixed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
with xyr rank demoted from Class C to Class B

Shouldn't it be the other way around? Class B to Class C?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I won't give away the whole of it obv, but an important thing to remember:

This is set in the AAPA canon during the 2030s. The 2030s were not a great time to be alive.

This is not a world you would want to be in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Different. Note that the number increments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, is this the same D-Class or are they different D-classes?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Easy +1 for interesting anomaly and testing logs exploring how it could Be used

I am sorta confused as to what Zenia wanted to get out for… or from what? The world? This reality?

This isn’t a complaint, it’s just left me with questions (which is probably a good sign of a well crafted narrative!)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The anomaly is interesting. I mean, a technique for removing eyes who works even in fiction… definitely interesting.

+1.

item.title: Staff Post - Closed
item.content:encoded:
Describing someone as non white for example would generally be considered offensive.

This is blatantly untrue, and frankly your entire post is a drawn-out ramble to justify your poorly disguised anti-transgender bigotry. Either accept that authors can give their characters whatever genders they desire, or this site won't accept you.

I'm locking this line of discussion as well to prevent pointless dogpiling.

This is a closed staff post. Do not reply to this post if you are not staff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is pretty incoherent, but if you're so caught off-guard by the use of nb character that the automatic response is to try and decrypt narrative clues for it — that's not really on the article, that's just a you thing. any unfamiliarity with it, while semi-understandable, isn't really a fault on the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Identities can exist independent of narrative. I sincerely have no clue what you're on about.

And yes, this has caused offense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hi, like I said, I genuinely don't mean to cause any offense. I'm not saying you can't have such characters without it being part of the story, but when you have 2 non binary characters it draws attention.

This wouldn't be a problem except that it implies something that, as far as I can tell, isn't there. This will be difficult to explain but I'll try and do so. Non binary is one of the few accepted ways of describing people where they are described by what they aren't, rather than what they are. Describing someone as non white for example would generally be considered offensive.

I say this in order to explain why when you say non binary, my brain automatically goes to binary, rather than non. Given this is an SCP article in always looking for the second layer so if I'm assuming that layer has something to do with binary I'm going to think this either relates to the relationship if the siblings, which it doesn't appear to, or it relates to the nebula.

I've never heard of a binary nebula before, and don't know what that would even be, but as a sci fi fan I have heard of binary star systems many times. I googled binary nebula and found reference to a discovery made a couple of years ago of a binary star system found inside a nebula.

Like I said, it's not that you can't have non binary characters, but in this instance in particular, having 2 non binary characters talking about space stuff, with the explanation being a mystery just leads me down the wrong road, and that's what I meant when I said it was distracting.

Hopefully I haven't caused any offense, as none was intended, but I hope I've explained well enough why this aspect took me out of the story somewhat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is wonderful, very well done!

item.title: Staff Post - Site Crit
item.content:encoded:
Kampfarsch,

As per the site rules:

Rule Zero: Don't be a dick. Every other rule follows from this.

This is a direct violation of this rule, and it will not be tolerated. You do not get to decide what are and are not "made up pronouns", nor do you get to decide what is or is not allowed in scientific reports.

Science neither backs up your bigotry, nor does it appreciate it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not the author, but a great deal of enby authors and writers visit this site, and seeing stuff like this which normalizes something so often characterized as deviant and attention-seeking in popular media is a really validating experience. Speaking from personal experience here, having such an integral part of someone's existence invalidated and shied away from in media depictions because it's too "strange" or "forced" only seeks to further alienate them and make them feel unwelcome when they just want to get by and live a normal, happy life.

Also, all pronouns are made up, that's how language works

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ok i dont want to start a thing but can we please not use made up pronouns in a scientific report its incredibly out of place

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They're non-binary because I'm non-binary and I wanted to write representation for my (and others') identity. It doesn't need story relevance because people's identities don't need justification (narrative or otherwise) to exist. It's also entirely possible for siblings to both be trans; it isn't some rare, mystical thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For story purposes yes but for an scp article that's designed for the purpose of being of use to the Foundation it should probably be there. The Foundation would consider it necessary, even if they also redacted it from "our" clearance level.

Also, and I genuinely mean no offense here, but having 2 non binary characters who are also siblings is odd. I'm sure it's not unheard of, but it's odd and a little distracting. I'm not sure if it had some relevance to the story that I missed. If so, fair enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't consider any of the steps as being necessary for the story. This isn't about how the anomaly works; it's about how it can be exploited, how it was exploited.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Something that bothers me about this is that, even though the technique is demonstrated throughout the article, the steps to perform it are unlisted, even in an expunged format.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You've got such a distinctive style that I recognized you were the author after two seconds of opening the page; just something interesting I noticed.

The article itself is cool af, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There is a fifth force which shaped religious belief, but its effect is so universal and profound that it deserves to stand alone.

This is, of course, space travel — and in any discussion of religion, it deserves to be written thus:

SPACE TRAVEL!


— Dune

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Surreal. Novote because I'm unsure how I like the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: gee nat how come ur jamcon lets u use two nb characters wait i did the same thing for 5kon. this was bizarre and left me feeling a little sad at the end for reasons i cant parse out. easy upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
♬ How's my summer ♬
♬ Did it cure your mind ♬
(video warning for flashing graphics)

The last of my Jamcon 2020 entries.

Alexandra and Zenia are non-binary and go by xe/xyr and zhe/zher pronouns, respectively.
